skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guide for designing, implementing, and evaluating MCP servers for external APIs using Python FastMCP or the TypeScript MCP SDK.
skill.summary: Build agent-friendly MCP servers with implementation patterns, evaluation guidance, and security-aware design practices.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but review shows most are false positives from MCP documentation, license text, and example commands. The real risk is the bundled evaluation harness, which can launch user-specified local MCP server commands and connect to user-specified remote MCP endpoints with credentials. No evidence found of malicious intent or prompt injection, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Command Execution in Evaluation Harness
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The evaluation CLI accepts a command, arguments, and environment values, then passes them to the stdio MCP client. This is expected for testing local MCP servers, but running untrusted commands could execute arbitrary code or expose secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command path is clear from CLI arguments into StdioServerParameters. The behavior is legitimate for an MCP evaluation tool, so the risk depends on whether users run trusted servers only.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Endpoint and Header Support
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The evaluation harness supports SSE and HTTP MCP transports with user-provided URLs and headers. This enables legitimate remote MCP testing, but users can send bearer tokens or other headers to any endpoint they configure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly accepts URL and header inputs and opens remote MCP transports. No malicious destination is hardcoded, which lowers severity but not the operational risk.

low_findings.title: Static Alerts Mostly Come From Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many command, URL, API key, and token alerts occur inside guides or example commands. They document MCP setup and evaluation workflows rather than hidden execution or exfiltration logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are prose or fenced examples using placeholder values such as example URLs and sample API keys. They do not show concealed network destinations or credential collection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match words in license text and MCP documentation headings. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms being implemented for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Sampled static locations contain ordinary text, not cryptographic code. I did not find semantic evidence that the skill performs weak cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Passed to Stdio MCP Client
dangerous_patterns.description: The harness forwards CLI command, args, and env values into StdioServerParameters. This is necessary for local MCP evaluation but should only be used with trusted commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution path is explicit and confirmed in code. It is a dual-use capability rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote URL and Header Forwarding
dangerous_patterns.description: The harness accepts remote MCP URLs and arbitrary headers for SSE or HTTP transports. Users should avoid passing secrets to untrusted endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly forwards user-supplied URL and header values. There is no hardcoded suspicious endpoint, so this is an operational warning.

content.value_statement: MCP servers need careful tool design, validation, and evaluation to work well with agents. This skill provides practical guidance for planning, building, and testing Python or TypeScript MCP integrations.
actual_capabilities: Explains agent-centered MCP tool design principles for real workflows.
Guides Python FastMCP and Node TypeScript MCP server implementation.
Provides reference material for schemas, pagination, errors, and response formats.
Includes an evaluation harness for testing MCP tools with Claude models.
Shows how to create XML evaluation tasks with verifiable expected answers.
Covers quality checklists for naming, validation, type safety, and documentation.
limitations: It is guidance and tooling, not a complete MCP server generator.
Remote documentation links may change after the skill was published.
The evaluation harness requires user-provided API keys and trusted MCP server commands.
Security review is still needed for any MCP server produced with the guide.

use_cases.title: Plan an API-backed MCP integration
use_cases.description: Map service documentation into agent-friendly tools, shared helpers, and response formats before implementation.

use_cases.title: Implement a typed MCP server
use_cases.description: Follow Python or TypeScript patterns for validation models, tool registration, async I/O, and error handling.

use_cases.title: Evaluate tool usefulness for agents
use_cases.description: Create realistic read-only evaluation questions and run them against an MCP server with the included harness.

prompt_templates.title: Choose MCP tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to review this API documentation and propose the first five MCP tools to build. Focus on complete user workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Design implementation structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to create a Python FastMCP implementation plan for this service. Include helpers, validation models, pagination, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review an MCP server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to review this MCP server for tool naming, schema quality, response limits, authentication handling, and agent usability.

prompt_templates.title: Create evaluation tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to create ten read-only evaluation questions for this MCP server. Each answer must be stable, verifiable, and specific.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a ticketing system API.
output: A prioritized list of workflow-level tools such as ticket search, ticket triage, and status update helpers.
A shared infrastructure plan covering authentication, pagination, response formatting, and error handling.
A testing plan with read-only evaluation questions and expected answers.

output_examples.input: Review my TypeScript MCP server before release.
output: A checklist-based review of tool descriptions, Zod schemas, strict TypeScript usage, and output limits.
Specific improvement notes for unclear parameters, oversized responses, and missing error guidance.
Recommended evaluation tasks to confirm the server works for realistic agent workflows.

best_practices: Design tools around complete user workflows, not single API endpoints.
Use strict validation models and clear field descriptions for every tool input.
Create read-only evaluations before release and use the results to improve tool design.
anti_patterns: Do not expose every API endpoint as a separate low-level tool without workflow design.
Do not return large raw API payloads when concise agent-ready output is enough.
Do not run the evaluation harness against untrusted commands or endpoints with real secrets.

faq.question: Does this skill create an MCP server automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides planning, implementation, review, and evaluation. The developer still writes and reviews the server code.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: It provides guidance for Python FastMCP and Node TypeScript implementations using the MCP SDK.

faq.question: Can it help with API authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains planning for authentication helpers and environment-based credentials, but users must protect their own secrets.

faq.question: Does it include evaluation tooling?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes scripts for connecting to MCP servers and running evaluation questions through Claude.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production credentials?
faq.answer: Use caution. Only pass production credentials to trusted local commands or trusted remote MCP endpoints.

faq.question: What makes a good MCP tool according to this skill?
faq.answer: A good tool supports a real workflow, has strict input validation, gives concise results, and returns actionable errors.
